heroOverview: Choleperitonitis is peritoneal inflammation caused by bile leaking into the abdominal cavity — most often from a ruptured or perforated gallbladder, a severed bile duct, or necrosis of the biliary wall. The incidence of bile-related peritonitis in adults undergoing laparoscopic cholecystectomy is reported at roughly 0.1-0.5% per procedure, but it also arises spontaneously from necrotising cholecystitis, gallbladder perforation, or biliary trauma. Bile is chemically caustic: glycodeoxycholic and taurodeoxycholic acid in escaped bile cause direct epithelial damage to the peritoneum within hours, producing chemical peritonitis even before bacteria colonise the fluid. This makes choleperitonitis a surgical emergency — untreated, the chemical injury progresses to bacterial superinfection, sepsis, and multi-organ failure. Early recognition and operative source control are the determinants of survival.

keyStats.prevalence: Gallbladder perforation with bile peritonitis occurs in approximately 0.5-10% of acute cholecystitis cases; bile duct leak after laparoscopic cholecystectomy in 0.1-0.5% of procedures
keyStats.demographics: Mirrors the demographics of gallstone disease: more common in women, adults over 40, those with obesity or diabetes
keyStats.avgAge: Most cases occur in adults over 40; postoperative bile leaks affect adults at any age who have had biliary surgery
keyStats.globalCases: Exact global incidence is unknown; gallstones affect approximately 10-15% of Western adults, with a small proportion developing complicated disease

definition: Choleperitonitis (ICD-10-CM: K65.3) is defined as peritonitis secondary to the presence of bile within the peritoneal cavity. It represents the intersection of biliary disease and peritoneal pathology. When bile escapes the biliary tract — through perforation of an acutely or gangrenously inflamed gallbladder, disruption of a bile duct during surgery or trauma, or erosion of a biliary-enteric fistula — it contacts the visceral and parietal peritoneum. The bile acids, phospholipids, and conjugated salts in bile exert direct cytotoxic effects, triggering a sterile chemical peritonitis that evolves rapidly. In up to 50% of cases, the bile is bacterially contaminated — most often with Escherichia coli, Klebsiella, Enterococcus, or Clostridium — converting chemical to infectious peritonitis within 24-72 hours. The condition is classified within the broader K65 peritonitis group but is distinguished by its biliary aetiology and the distinctive greenish or golden-brown peritoneal fluid found at operation. Gastroenterology and hepatobiliary surgery jointly manage most cases, with interventional radiology playing a growing role in source control for postoperative leaks. Choleperitonitis is categorised as a complicated biliary disease and carries a mortality of 10-30% depending on the degree of contamination, patient comorbidity, and time to intervention.

typesClassification.description: The most common form; acute or gangrenous cholecystitis erodes the gallbladder wall, releasing infected bile into the peritoneum. A free perforation (Niemeier type I) produces generalised peritonitis, the most dangerous form.

Bile duct injury, clip slippage from the cystic duct stump, or duct-of-Luschka leak after cholecystectomy releases bile into the abdominal cavity; ranges from localised bile collection to generalised peritonitis.
Blunt or penetrating abdominal trauma ruptures the gallbladder or bile ducts, releasing bile; frequently coexists with other abdominal organ injuries.
Rare; seen in neonates and infants from congenital bile duct abnormalities, and occasionally in adults with biliary malignancy or erosion of a biliary stent.

primarySymptoms: Severe, diffuse abdominal pain that is often described as burning or chemical in quality, spreading rapidly from the right upper quadrant to involve the entire abdomen as bile distributes through the peritoneal cavity.
Abdominal rigidity — the abdomen becomes board-like and exquisitely tender to touch, reflecting peritoneal irritation from bile acid exposure.
Nausea and persistent vomiting, often the first symptoms recognised before the abdominal pain becomes truly global.
High fever above 38.5°C with rigors, indicating systemic absorption of bile components or early bacterial superinfection of the peritoneal fluid.
Tachycardia with a heart rate typically above 100 beats per minute, reflecting early haemodynamic compromise from third-space fluid losses into the inflamed peritoneum.
Abdominal distension from ileus — the intestines halt their normal peristalsis in response to peritoneal contamination, causing progressive bloating.
Rebound tenderness — pain that sharply worsens when the examining hand is quickly removed from the abdomen, a clinical sign of parietal peritoneal inflammation.
Jaundice or yellow discolouration of the skin and sclerae in cases where the common bile duct is also obstructed or damaged.
Referred right shoulder tip pain due to diaphragmatic irritation by bile that has tracked up into the right subphrenic space.
Hypotension and signs of septic shock in advanced cases — confusion, cold clammy extremities, oliguria, and systolic blood pressure below 90 mmHg.
earlyWarningSigns: Acute right upper quadrant pain that suddenly changes character and spreads to involve the entire abdomen.
A known or suspected diagnosis of acute cholecystitis with abrupt worsening despite several hours of antibiotics.
Fever with a new fall in blood pressure or escalating heart rate after biliary surgery.
Greenish discolouration or bile-stained fluid draining from an abdominal wound, drain, or surgical site.
emergencySigns: Board-like abdominal rigidity with generalised tenderness — a classic sign of free peritoneal contamination requiring emergency surgical assessment.
Hypotension (systolic below 90 mmHg), confusion, or oliguria in a patient with abdominal pain, indicating septic shock.
Absence of bowel sounds with marked abdominal distension in the context of known biliary disease.
Rapidly rising fever and white cell count with abdominal pain after recent biliary surgery or trauma.
Bile-coloured output from a drain with haemodynamic instability, indicating active ongoing bile leak.

causes.description: Sustained occlusion of the cystic duct by a gallstone leads to gallbladder distension, ischaemia, and necrosis of the wall; in approximately 0.5-10% of acute cholecystitis cases the wall perforates, releasing infected bile directly into the peritoneal space.

The most common cause in the modern surgical era; inadvertent clipping or division of the common bile duct or right hepatic duct during laparoscopic or open cholecystectomy causes bile to accumulate in the abdomen; even a small duct-of-Luschka leak can produce peritonitis if undetected.

Motor vehicle accidents, high-velocity blunt injury, or stabbing can rupture the gallbladder or transect bile ducts; because the bile is often sterile at the time of injury, the initial peritonitis is chemical and becomes bacterially contaminated within 24-48 hours.

A cholecysto-enteric or cholecystopericolic fistula formed by erosion of a gallstone through the gallbladder wall can rupture or leak, releasing bile and sometimes bowel content simultaneously into the peritoneum.

Full-thickness gangrene of the gallbladder wall, which occurs in approximately 2-30% of acute cholecystitis and more often in diabetic patients or the critically ill, can perforate without the sentinel of preceding colicky pain, making diagnosis delayed.

Metal or plastic biliary stents placed for malignant or benign obstruction occasionally migrate, erode the bile duct wall, or become blocked and cause pressure necrosis, leading to bile leak into the peritoneal or retroperitoneal space.

Spontaneous perforation of the extrahepatic bile ducts is a rare but well-recognised cause of neonatal jaundice and bile ascites; the exact mechanism is uncertain but includes congenital weakness, inspissated bile, and anomalous duct anatomy.

riskFactors.description: Gallstones are the principal driver of acute cholecystitis and its complications; approximately 10-15% of Western adults have gallstones, and the lifetime risk of complications is about 15-25% in those with symptomatic disease.

Diabetes increases the risk of gangrenous and acalculous cholecystitis through vascular disease and neuropathy, which impair gallbladder wall healing and pain perception, delaying presentation and increasing perforation risk by two- to fourfold.

Older adults have more vascular disease, higher rates of acalculous cholecystitis, and delayed presentation, making gallbladder perforation and subsequent bile peritonitis significantly more common after age 65.

Cholecystectomy — performed on approximately 1.2 million Americans annually — carries a small but definite risk of bile leak, particularly if there has been intraoperative bleeding, anatomical variation, or difficult dissection.
High-velocity motor vehicle accidents and penetrating injuries are leading causes of traumatic biliary disruption; pre-hospital immobilisation and rapid trauma assessment reduce time to diagnosis.

riskFactors.factor: Prolonged fasting and total parenteral nutrition
riskFactors.description: Fasting and TPN reduce gallbladder contractility, promoting bile stasis and the development of acalculous cholecystitis in critically ill or hospitalised patients, which can progress to gangrene and perforation.
Obesity increases cholesterol saturation of bile, gallstone formation, and the risk of acute cholecystitis; it also makes surgery technically more difficult, raising the rate of bile duct injury.

affectedDemographics: Women aged 40-70 with gallstone disease, who have the highest absolute burden of biliary complications
Adults over 65, in whom gangrenous cholecystitis and delayed diagnosis increase perforation risk
Patients with diabetes, particularly those on insulin or with autonomic neuropathy
Critically ill patients on total parenteral nutrition, at risk of acalculous cholecystitis
Patients who have had laparoscopic cholecystectomy within the preceding days to weeks
Trauma patients with high-velocity blunt abdominal injury
diagnosisOverview: The diagnosis of choleperitonitis is primarily clinical and operative: a patient with known biliary disease or recent biliary surgery who develops peritoneal signs — diffuse tenderness, guarding, and rigidity — has biliary peritonitis until proven otherwise. The history focuses on symptom timing relative to any recent biliary surgery or trauma, previous cholecystitis episodes, fever duration, and haemodynamic stability. Laboratory findings typically show a leucocytosis above 12,000/μL, elevated CRP, and often raised bilirubin and alkaline phosphatase. Abdominal ultrasound is the first imaging study: it can identify a ruptured or thickened gallbladder, free peritoneal fluid, pneumoperitoneum, and dilated bile ducts. CT of the abdomen with contrast is the primary imaging modality — it delineates the extent of contamination, identifies bile collections (bilomas), and locates the source. The characteristic CT finding is free fluid of relatively high attenuation (bile) in the right subphrenic space, right paracolic gutter, and pelvis. In postoperative cases, MRCP or ERCP defines the anatomy of the bile duct and identifies the leak site. Diagnostic paracentesis revealing bile-stained fluid confirms the diagnosis. Key differential diagnoses include other causes of acute peritonitis — perforated duodenal ulcer, appendix perforation, and ischaemic bowel — which require equally urgent surgical management.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line emergency imaging to identify gallbladder distension, wall thickening, perforation, pericholecystic fluid, and free intra-abdominal fluid consistent with bile peritonitis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A gel is applied to the abdomen and a probe moved over it; painless, about 15-20 minutes, and performed at the bedside in unstable patients. No special preparation is needed in an emergency.

diagnosticTests.test: Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis
diagnosticTests.purpose: Delineates the extent of peritoneal contamination, identifies bilomas, gallbladder anatomy, bile duct integrity, and complications such as hepatic abscess or bowel obstruction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An intravenous iodinated contrast injection is given; the scan takes a few minutes and results are reviewed by the surgeon and radiologist together within the same hour in most acute centres.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count, CRP, and serum bilirubin
diagnosticTests.purpose: Leucocytosis confirms systemic inflammatory response; elevated bilirubin and alkaline phosphatase suggest biliary origin; serial CRP monitors treatment response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw from the arm; results available within 1-2 hours in acute care.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive imaging of the biliary tree to identify the exact location and extent of duct injury, biloma, or leak — essential planning for ERCP or surgery.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30-45 minute MRI scan with no radiation and no contrast in most cases; claustrophobic patients may need sedation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Both diagnostic and therapeutic: identifies the bile leak site and can place a stent to bridge the defect, diverting bile flow away from the peritoneum.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under sedation; a flexible scope is passed through the mouth into the duodenum and into the bile duct. A stent or clip can be applied in the same session. Takes 30-60 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Sampling of peritoneal fluid to confirm bile content (high bilirubin in the fluid relative to serum) and to obtain culture and sensitivity for antibiotic guidance.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A needle is inserted into the abdomen under local anaesthetic or ultrasound guidance; a few millilitres of fluid are withdrawn. Takes about 10-15 minutes.

treatmentOverview: Choleperitonitis is managed on three simultaneous fronts: haemodynamic resuscitation, antibiotic cover, and source control. Intravenous fluid resuscitation with crystalloids is started immediately, targeting a urine output above 0.5 mL/kg per hour and normalising heart rate and blood pressure. Broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics covering Gram-negative bacilli and anaerobes — typically piperacillin-tazobactam or a carbapenem in high-risk patients — are given as soon as blood cultures are drawn, following Surviving Sepsis guidelines. Source control is the definitive treatment and must not be delayed for more than six to twelve hours once the diagnosis is established. For gallbladder perforation, emergency cholecystectomy (usually laparoscopic where feasible) removes the source and allows thorough peritoneal lavage. When the patient is too unstable for general anaesthesia, percutaneous ultrasound-guided cholecystostomy — tube drainage of the gallbladder — can temporise until definitive surgery is possible. For postoperative bile duct leaks, ERCP with biliary stent placement is the treatment of choice in haemodynamically stable patients, with a success rate of 80-90% for minor leaks; major duct injuries require operative repair by a hepatobiliary surgeon. Peritoneal lavage — irrigation of the abdominal cavity with warm normal saline during surgery — reduces the bacterial and chemical load. Post-operatively, patients require ICU-level monitoring for 24-48 hours, continued antibiotics guided by cultures, and nutritional support. Delayed interval cholecystectomy may be needed after percutaneous drainage, typically four to six weeks later once inflammation resolves.

medicalTreatments.name: Broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics
medicalTreatments.description: Piperacillin-tazobactam (4.5 g IV every 8 hours) or a carbapenem for high-risk patients; covers E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterococcus, and anaerobes. Narrowed after 48-72 hours based on culture sensitivities. Continued for 4-7 days after source control.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces sepsis mortality by approximately 7-10% per hour when administered within 1 hour of suspected sepsis, per Surviving Sepsis data.

medicalTreatments.name: IV crystalloid resuscitation
medicalTreatments.description: Balanced crystalloids (Ringer's lactate or Hartmann's) at 20-30 mL/kg bolus initially, then titrated to haemodynamic targets; vasopressors added if hypotension persists after adequate fluid loading.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Restores haemodynamic stability in approximately 60-70% of septic peritonitis patients when combined with early source control.

medicalTreatments.name: Biliary stent via ERCP
medicalTreatments.description: Endoscopic placement of a plastic or metal stent across a bile duct leak diverts bile flow into the duodenum, allowing the peritoneal leak site to heal. First-line for postoperative bile leaks without duct transaction.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Successfully controls bile leak in approximately 80-90% of minor cystic duct stump or peripheral duct leaks when performed within 48-72 hours.

medicalTreatments.name: Percutaneous cholecystostomy
medicalTreatments.description: Ultrasound or CT-guided insertion of a drainage tube into the gallbladder; decompresses the inflamed gallbladder and reduces ongoing bile leakage in patients too unwell for immediate surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves clinical stabilisation in approximately 70-80% of high-surgical-risk patients with acute cholecystitis, bridging to definitive cholecystectomy.

medicalTreatments.name: Analgesics (IV opioids and paracetamol)
medicalTreatments.description: Adequate pain control with morphine or fentanyl IV, combined with regular paracetamol, reduces physiological stress response and facilitates clinical assessment without masking signs of deterioration.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provides clinically meaningful pain reduction (≥3 point reduction on visual analogue scale) in over 85% of patients within 30 minutes of IV opioid administration.

surgicalOptions.name: Emergency laparoscopic or open cholecystectomy with peritoneal lavage
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of the ruptured or gangrenous gallbladder and thorough irrigation of the peritoneum with warm saline to remove bile, bacteria, and fibrin. Laparoscopic approach is preferred when technically feasible.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Overall survival in non-septic patients over 85-90%; mortality rises to 10-30% with established sepsis or multi-organ failure depending on comorbidity.

surgicalOptions.name: Hepatobiliary duct repair or reconstruction
surgicalOptions.description: For major bile duct injuries (Strasberg type E), Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy performed by a specialist hepatobiliary surgeon restores biliary-enteric continuity. Timing depends on peritoneal soiling — immediate in clean fields, delayed 4-6 weeks in contaminated fields.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Long-term success of hepaticojejunostomy for major duct injuries exceeds 80-90% at specialist centres.

alternativeTreatments.name: Image-guided percutaneous drainage of biloma
alternativeTreatments.description: Ultrasound or CT-guided drainage of a walled-off bile collection can adequately manage contained postoperative bile leaks without open surgery, particularly when the peritoneal contamination is localised.

alternativeTreatments.name: Nutritional support during recovery
alternativeTreatments.description: Enteral nutrition via nasogastric tube is preferred over parenteral nutrition for maintaining gut barrier function after peritoneal contamination; early enteral feeding within 24-48 hours of surgery reduces infection complications.

whySeeSpecialist: Choleperitonitis must be managed by a team that includes a general or hepatobiliary surgeon, an interventional gastroenterologist, an intensivist, and an interventional radiologist. A hepatobiliary surgeon with experience in bile duct reconstruction is essential when major duct injury is suspected, because primary repair of a bile duct injury is associated with a significantly higher re-stenosis rate than formal Roux-en-Y reconstruction. Referral to a specialist centre within 24-48 hours of diagnosis prevents the long-term complications of biliary stricture, which develop in up to 30% of patients managed at non-specialist sites.
doctorSelectionGuide: Any patient with suspected choleperitonitis should be assessed immediately in an emergency surgical department. Look for a centre with 24-hour access to hepatobiliary surgery, interventional endoscopy, interventional radiology, and ICU. For the elective management of bile duct injuries following the acute phase, seek a hepatobiliary surgeon who performs Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy at a minimum volume of 20 cases per year, as outcomes correlate strongly with surgical experience in this high-complexity procedure.
preventionStrategies: Treat symptomatic gallstones promptly with elective cholecystectomy before they progress to acute cholecystitis and its complications.
Ensure laparoscopic cholecystectomy is performed by adequately trained surgeons and converted early to open technique when anatomy is unclear.
In patients with acute cholecystitis, early surgery within 72 hours reduces the risk of gallbladder necrosis and perforation compared with delayed management.
Use routine intraoperative cholangiography to display biliary anatomy and reduce bile duct injury rates.
In critically ill patients, maintain enteral feeding where possible to preserve gallbladder contractility and prevent acalculous cholecystitis.
Manage diabetes and obesity, which are modifiable risk factors for gangrenous cholecystitis and perforation.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain a healthy weight: obesity sharply raises gallstone risk and complicates surgery.
Follow a low-fat, high-fibre diet to reduce cholesterol supersaturation of bile and gallstone formation.
Stay well hydrated; dehydration concentrates bile and promotes stone formation.
Avoid rapid weight loss or very-low-calorie diets, which mobilise cholesterol and increase stone formation risk.
Attend follow-up appointments after any biliary surgery; early reporting of abdominal pain, fever, or jaundice allows detection of bile leaks before peritonitis develops.
Report right upper quadrant pain, fever, or jaundice promptly rather than waiting — these symptoms after cholecystectomy indicate a biliary complication.

recommended: High-fibre diet with fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and legumes to reduce biliary cholesterol saturation
Low-fat diet — saturated fat intake below 10% of total calories — to reduce gallbladder stress and gallstone risk
Plenty of water (at least 1.5-2 litres daily) to keep bile dilute
Plant-based proteins and polyunsaturated fats, which are associated with lower gallstone incidence
avoid: High-fat meals, particularly saturated and trans fats, which provoke gallbladder contraction and increase risk of colic
Rapid weight loss programmes or very-low-calorie diets without medical supervision, which raise stone formation risk
Excessive refined carbohydrates and sugar, associated with elevated biliary cholesterol
Alcohol excess, which promotes liver disease and changes bile composition

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate-intensity exercise — 150 minutes per week as recommended by WHO — reduces gallstone risk by approximately 30-40% through weight management and improved bile flow. During the acute phase of choleperitonitis and for four to six weeks after surgery, physical activity is restricted to gentle walking; strenuous activity risks wound dehiscence or hernia. Return to full activity is guided by surgical healing and the absence of complications.
dailyManagement: After biliary surgery, monitor daily for fever above 38°C, increasing abdominal pain, jaundice, or drainage from the wound — all warrant immediate surgical review.
Take prescribed antibiotics for the full course, even when feeling better, to prevent recurrence of infection.
If a drain is in place at home, record the colour and volume of output and contact the surgical team if it turns bilious (green-yellow) again.
Maintain adequate hydration and caloric intake to support healing; oral nutritional supplements can fill gaps during early recovery.
Attend all hepatobiliary follow-up imaging appointments, as stricture of a repaired bile duct can develop silently over months.
Recognise the signs of late bile duct stricture — deepening jaundice, recurrent fever, right upper quadrant discomfort — and seek review within 24 hours.
prognosis: The outlook for choleperitonitis depends on the cause, the degree of peritoneal contamination, the patient's baseline health, and the speed of intervention. In otherwise healthy adults with a postoperative bile leak managed within 24-48 hours by ERCP stenting, prognosis is excellent with full recovery expected. Gallbladder perforation with generalised peritonitis carries a reported mortality of 10-30% in published series, driven by septic shock, underlying diabetes, and delayed diagnosis. When source control is achieved within six hours of onset, 30-day survival exceeds 85% in most studies. Long-term complications — bile duct stricture, recurrent cholangitis, and liver abscess — can develop months to years later in patients with major bile duct injury, and lifelong specialist follow-up is warranted. Infants with spontaneous bile duct perforation have a good prognosis with prompt surgical repair, with survival exceeding 90% in contemporary series.
recoveryTimeline: After successful ERCP stenting for a minor bile leak, most patients are discharged within three to five days and resume normal activity within two to three weeks; the stent is removed endoscopically at six to eight weeks. After emergency cholecystectomy for gallbladder perforation, hospital stay ranges from five to fourteen days depending on contamination extent and comorbidity; return to light activity in two to four weeks and full activity in six to eight weeks. Formal bile duct reconstruction (Roux-en-Y) requires a longer recovery of two to four weeks in hospital and six to twelve weeks before return to work.
complications: Septic shock and multi-organ failure — bile contamination drives systemic inflammation; untreated within hours it progresses to renal failure, respiratory failure, and cardiovascular collapse.
Intra-abdominal abscess — residual contamination can form walled-off collections in the subphrenic space, pelvis, or between bowel loops, requiring percutaneous or surgical drainage.
Bile duct stricture — repair or inflammation of injured bile ducts can produce narrowing over months to years, causing progressive jaundice, cholangitis, and secondary biliary cirrhosis.
Enteric fistula — bile and inflammation can erode into adjacent bowel, producing a biliary-enteric fistula requiring further surgery.
Wound infection and incisional hernia — major abdominal contamination increases wound complication rates; hernias are common after open peritoneal lavage.

Chronic abdominal adhesions — extensive peritoneal inflammation produces fibrous adhesions between loops of bowel, which are the leading cause of small bowel obstruction in the years following peritonitis.

supportResources.description: Patient-facing information on gallstone disease, cholecystitis, and when to seek care from the U.S. National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases.

supportResources.name: NHS — Peritonitis
supportResources.description: Plain-English UK guidance on peritonitis causes, symptoms, treatment, and when to call emergency services.

supportResources.name: British Society of Gastroenterology — Acute Cholecystitis Guidance
supportResources.description: Clinical guidance from the British Society of Gastroenterology on the management of acute cholecystitis and its complications.

sources.title: Niemeier OW. Acute Free Perforation of the Gallbladder. Annals of Surgery, 1934 (seminal classification paper)
Strasberg SM, Hertl M, Soper NJ. An Analysis of the Problem of Biliary Injury During Laparoscopic Cholecystectomy. Journal of the American College of Surgeons, 1995

confusedWithConditions.name: Generalised acute peritonitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Generalised peritonitis (K65.0) refers to any bacterial peritonitis regardless of cause; choleperitonitis specifically requires bile as the causative fluid, producing the distinctive bilious fluid found at surgery or paracentesis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: SBP occurs in cirrhotic patients with ascites and no identifiable surgical source; choleperitonitis has a distinct biliary source of contamination and is managed surgically rather than with antibiotics alone.

confusedWithConditions.name: Complicated gallstone disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Gallstones cause biliary colic and cholecystitis without peritoneal involvement; choleperitonitis specifically denotes bile leakage outside the biliary tree, a far more serious and distinct clinical emergency.

confusedWithConditions.name: Peritoneal abscess
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A peritoneal abscess is a walled-off collection; choleperitonitis is an active, diffuse, or rapidly spreading bile peritonitis requiring urgent source control rather than drainage alone.

relatedConditions.name: Generalised acute peritonitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Choleperitonitis is a specific subtype within the broader peritonitis family; management principles overlap significantly.

relatedConditions.name: Peritoneal abscess
relatedConditions.relevance: A late sequela of choleperitonitis; walled-off bile and bacterial contamination can form abscesses requiring separate drainage.

relatedConditions.name: Gallstone disease
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common underlying cause of gallbladder perforation leading to choleperitonitis.

relatedConditions.name: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares the peritonitis mechanism but has a different cause and management pathway, making distinction important clinically.

faqs.question: What is choleperitonitis?
faqs.answer: Choleperitonitis is peritoneal inflammation caused by bile leaking into the abdominal cavity, most often from a ruptured gallbladder or a bile duct injury. It is a surgical emergency because bile is chemically caustic and quickly becomes infected.

faqs.question: What causes bile to leak into the abdomen?
faqs.answer: The most common causes are gallbladder perforation from severe cholecystitis, a bile duct injury during cholecystectomy, and abdominal trauma. In rare cases, spontaneous duct perforation or biliary stent erosion is responsible.

faqs.question: How is choleperitonitis treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment requires simultaneous resuscitation with IV fluids, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and urgent source control. For gallbladder rupture, emergency surgery is performed. For postoperative bile leaks, an ERCP stent can seal the leak without an operation.

faqs.question: Is choleperitonitis life-threatening?
faqs.answer: Yes. Untreated, bile peritonitis progresses to septic shock and multi-organ failure. Mortality in patients with generalised bile peritonitis is 10-30%, depending on comorbidity and time to surgery.

faqs.question: What are the signs of a bile leak after gallbladder surgery?
faqs.answer: Warning signs include persistent abdominal pain after cholecystectomy, fever, jaundice, and bile-coloured (green-yellow) drainage from a wound or drain. Any of these symptoms after biliary surgery require same-day surgical review.

faqs.question: How is a bile leak diagnosed after surgery?
faqs.answer: CT scan usually detects free fluid or a bile collection. MRCP maps the biliary tree to find the leak site. A drain bilirubin level much higher than serum bilirubin confirms bile in the collected fluid.

faqs.question: Can a bile leak be treated without an operation?
faqs.answer: Minor postoperative bile leaks from the cystic duct stump or small ducts are often treated with ERCP stent placement, with success in 80-90% of cases. Major bile duct transection requires surgical reconstruction.

faqs.question: How long is recovery after surgery for choleperitonitis?
faqs.answer: After ERCP stenting for a minor leak, most patients are discharged in three to five days. After emergency surgery for gallbladder perforation, hospital stay is five to fourteen days, with return to light activity in two to four weeks.

faqs.question: What antibiotics are used for choleperitonitis?
faqs.answer: Piperacillin-tazobactam or a carbapenem is the standard first-line IV antibiotic, covering E. coli, Klebsiella, and anaerobes. The regimen is narrowed after 48-72 hours based on culture results.

faqs.question: Can gallstones cause bile peritonitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct cause acute cholecystitis, which can progress to gallbladder wall gangrene and perforation, releasing infected bile into the abdomen. This is the most common cause of choleperitonitis.

faqs.question: What is the difference between choleperitonitis and regular peritonitis?
faqs.answer: Regular peritonitis can result from any abdominal contamination. Choleperitonitis specifically involves bile leakage, which adds a chemical component to the peritoneal injury on top of any bacterial infection.

faqs.question: Does everyone with gallstones need to worry about this condition?
faqs.answer: No. Gallbladder perforation occurs in only 0.5-10% of acute cholecystitis cases. Elective cholecystectomy for symptomatic gallstones performed before complications arise prevents it.

faqs.question: What long-term problems can follow choleperitonitis?
faqs.answer: Bile duct stricture, recurrent cholangitis, abdominal adhesions causing bowel obstruction, and incisional hernia are the main long-term risks. Specialist follow-up with imaging is needed after major bile duct injury.

faqs.question: How do surgeons wash out bile during an operation?
faqs.answer: Peritoneal lavage involves irrigating the abdominal cavity with several litres of warm normal saline during surgery to dilute and remove bile, bacteria, and fibrin clots, reducing the risk of post-operative abscess.

faqs.question: Why does bile damage the abdomen?
faqs.answer: Bile contains bile acids — including glycodeoxycholic and taurodeoxycholic acid — that are detergents. They disrupt cell membranes on contact, causing direct tissue injury to the peritoneum independently of any bacterial infection.

faqs.question: Can choleperitonitis happen in babies?
faqs.answer: Yes. Spontaneous perforation of the extrahepatic bile ducts is a rare but recognised cause of jaundice and bile ascites in neonates. Outcomes are good when identified early and treated surgically.

faqs.question: What should I do if I have right-sided abdominal pain after gallbladder surgery?
faqs.answer: Contact your surgical team the same day. Right upper quadrant pain, fever, or jaundice after cholecystectomy may indicate a bile leak. Do not wait for a routine follow-up appointment.

faqs.question: Is choleperitonitis the same as cholecystitis?
faqs.answer: No. Cholecystitis is inflammation confined to the gallbladder. Choleperitonitis occurs when the gallbladder ruptures or bile leaks outside the biliary tract into the peritoneal cavity, which is a far more serious complication.

faqs.question: Can I prevent bile peritonitis after gallbladder surgery?
faqs.answer: Intraoperative cholangiography to display biliary anatomy, early conversion to open surgery when anatomy is unclear, and prompt reporting of symptoms after discharge reduce but cannot eliminate the risk of bile leak and peritonitis.

faqs.question: What is a biloma?
faqs.answer: A biloma is a collection of bile outside the bile ducts, walled off within the abdominal cavity. It forms when a bile leak is contained by surrounding tissues rather than spreading freely. Small bilomas can be drained percutaneously without open surgery.

metaDescription: Choleperitonitis is peritonitis from bile leaking into the abdomen, often after gallbladder rupture or bile duct injury. Learn symptoms, treatment and outlook.
